title_en: Female Companions and the Mothers of the Believers
content_en:
Al-Isti'ab dedicates a substantial section to female Companions — women who met the Prophet (peace be upon him), believed in him, and transmitted hadith or participated in the life of the early Muslim community. This section is an invaluable resource for understanding the religious and social role of women in the formative period of Islam, and it challenges many stereotypes about the status of women in early Islamic society. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr's respectful and detailed treatment of female Companions reflects the high regard in which Islamic scholarship has always held the women of the prophetic generation.

The most eminent of the female Companions are the Mothers of the Believers (ummahat al-mu'minin) — the wives of the Prophet (peace be upon him), who are given this title in the Quran: 'The Prophet is closer to the believers than their own selves, and his wives are their mothers' (33:6). Their special status derives from their direct proximity to the Prophet, their role as transmitters of hadith about his private conduct and personal life, and the reverence owed them as his household. Al-Isti'ab's entries on each of the Mothers of the Believers are among the most detailed in the book.

Khadija bint Khuwaylid has already been discussed. After her death, the Prophet married several women, each of whom brought a distinct contribution to the early community. 'Aisha bint Abi Bakr is arguably the most significant female scholar in Islamic history. Her entry in Al-Isti'ab records that she transmitted over two thousand hadiths — a number exceeded only by a handful of the most prolific male Companions — and that scholars from across the Islamic world traveled to Medina to learn from her. She was particularly authoritative on matters of the Prophet's personal conduct, worship, and family life, giving her unique standing as a source for the Sunnah. The Prophet himself is reported to have said: 'Take half your religion from this red-cheeked one,' referring to 'Aisha.

Umm Salama (Hind bint Abi Umayya) is another of the Mothers of the Believers treated with considerable detail in Al-Isti'ab. She was among the early emigrants to Abyssinia, endured the death of her first husband Abu Salama (who died of wounds from the Battle of Uhud), and became the Prophet's wife in the fourth year AH. Her long life — she died around 62 AH — meant that she witnessed many of the major events of early Islam and was sought out for her knowledge well into the Umayyad period. Her entry records numerous hadiths and illustrates her role as an authoritative source on matters that only the Prophet's wives could witness directly.

Beyond the Mothers of the Believers, Al-Isti'ab documents the contributions of many other female Companions. Al-Khansa' bint 'Amr, the celebrated pre-Islamic poetess who accepted Islam late in the Prophet's life, is noted for her extraordinary fortitude: at the Battle of al-Qadisiyya, she sent her four sons to fight and, when all four were killed in a single day, praised Allah and expressed satisfaction that they had died as martyrs. Fatima bint Muhammad, the Prophet's daughter and the wife of 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, occupies a special place — she is described as the leader of the women of Paradise, and her brief life (she died only months after her father) is treated with great sensitivity. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr documents the Prophetic hadith about Fatima with care, acknowledging her unique status in the prophetic household and in Islamic piety more broadly.

The female Companions of Al-Isti'ab collectively demonstrate that Islamic women from the earliest generation were not passive recipients of religious teaching but active participants in the transmission of knowledge, the practice of worship, the conduct of war (in supporting roles and occasionally in combat), and the building of community. Their biographies in Al-Isti'ab stand as enduring testimony to the full participation of women in the religious life of the first Muslim generation — a participation that subsequent Islamic scholarship has consistently recognized, however imperfectly later historical conditions reflected it.
content_ar: أمهات المؤمنين لهن فضل عظيم لصحبتهن النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم ورواياتهن لأحاديثه الشريفة، وقد أفردهن ابن عبد البر بذكر خاص.
